data.text:
INT: --Verstandig. Yes, we were talking about your departure from Przemysl in April around about that Pesach time, 1940. 

MV: Yeah. I-- Przemysl was a border town between the Soviet Russia and the Germans. I observed that not far from the station-- outside there was, there was an officer working, an officer working outside, was outside. 

There were a couple of Russian officers and three German officers. And people were registering there to-- people from the army, from the-- these invalids and people who were before members of the Polish army who had demobilized. 

I mean, their units would disappear. They were, that is like they were registering themselves to go back to Poland. Some were wearing uniforms and-- and remnants of uniforms. Others were in civil clothes, and so on. 

So Poles-- going back. I found among the papers, which we took with them a whole-- a whole suitcase of different papers, which we-- and then we emptied a desk before leaving [NON-ENGLISH] there were a lot of papers which belonged to the people employed in [NON-ENGLISH]. 

Among them, I found, I found an identity card without a picture. Most of identity cards in Poland were without pictures-- only a description-- how high you are, and so on. 

So I found, I had it-- I think it was some-- [NON-ENGLISH] or something like that, a name like that. The description was similar. The age was similar. So I took this, I decided that I'm going home that way. 

At the same time, the Germans, the Russians advise Poles, and Jews-- whoever were-- people who came as refugees-- that they should either register themselves to accept Soviet citizenship, or they could register themselves to go back. 

Now, Jews did not want to accept Soviet citizenship, because they were afraid they will never be able to go back. And the war may be finished in a few weeks' time, and they live in France, and England. The English, will start the offensive. 

The people didn't want to accept their citizenship. And I thought, why should I wait so long here, along here. The best way to go will be I will go as a Polish soldier or officer back to Mielec. 

So I took my, I took, I knew that I will have two suitcases, so I went over there, I registered myself to go back that time from a Polish unit, so and so. I felt if I used the name of-- I made sure the types of units was there from a division like that. 

They didn't make any, they didn't make any fuss. They just put the name on, and they asked me how many pieces of luggage we have. I told them, four. They gave me four tickets, because I had to plan how to do it. 

So I, in the afternoon, I was allowed on the train. I was on the train back to Mielec. But suddenly I realize that we are going-- instead of going west, we are going northwest, north. 

And I think it was in [NON-ENGLISH]. Wherever it was, I think it was a place like that-- I think [NON-ENGLISH]-- that they started-- Poles, on the station, when the station stops, they started to shout-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- People who are healthy will go to Germany for work, will go to Germany. 

And then I realized that the doors are locked. And I realize that in the last, in the last carriage were German soldiers. And the moment the train stopped, they all were down on both sides, watching the train. 

So I decided that I will have to jump the train. I took three, four tickets-- because I decided not to go to Mielec, with, with two, with two. I said, I decided that before I go to Mielec, I will have to give away my two suitcases in somebody else's trust and change the tickets and go down a station before Mielec or after Mielec and then go back. 

Because if I come back with two suitcases, everybody-- I mean, it will draw attention that somebody came from that side without, somebody came from that side on a false name. 

So I befriended a man next to me-- he was an invalid, a sergeant, an invalid. And I told him, look, I will have to jump train. Here are your two blank tickets. Write out your name, take-- take off mine. 

I, he was from Cracow. He gave me his address. I will come to pick them up. And I've got a dinner jacket, a smoking-- a dinner jacket in here. The dinner jacket will be yours. He was very, very pleased with me. 

But now the question of how-- how to do it, because people-- the-- the-- the-- the doors were locked. But I spotted that the south-- when I was-- but I could move the-- the window down. 

So I spotted that when the train makes-- slows down and goes around, it goes not straight, but it has to go, it has to make the curve, pieces of coal were being thrown out from the engine to be collected by somebody. 

So when the train goes like that, they threw out, the trains from the outside. So that people, that the, the soldiers from the last, from the last carriage couldn't see it. When it came the other way, they threw out the coals from the other way. 

Now, I decided that when the train, train will slow down, it will be evening, I will jump out then. This is how I did. I jumped out, I walked in the forest. Oh, there was a forest not far from [NON-ENGLISH] or something like that, not far from [NON-ENGLISH]. 

I walked out, and I didn't know whether it was [NON-ENGLISH] or not. I think it was [NON-ENGLISH]. I walked out, and I walked and walked for a couple of hours in the dark. I knew that the lights were there. 

The Germans-- although it was war, but it was far away. You see, the city lights were on. So I walked in the opposite direction. I walked for about an hour, an hour and a half or two hours until I found, I found a little cottage. 

I knocked the door. I told him-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- It was the common greeting in Poland. I told him the truth-- that I'm an officer, that I jumped the train, because they are taking the people to Germany, and I want to-- I want to, I want to sleep here for the night, and I'll go away tomorrow morning. 

And this is what I did. He-- he-- he-- he-- he accepted me. He accepted me. We were eating breakfast in the morning-- I told him, and I-- he-- I could see that he has got a daughter. 

She was 17 years old. And she looked-- my God, she looked like a typical Pole-- blond and blue eyes, you know, with this face. And I said to him, look, I'll tell you something-- why don't we take, you have a horse, the cart. Why don't we take this cart and you give me a lift to town. 

And for money-- I had money prepared-- and for money, you will have a nice meal and a good time, and a good time, and later, I will go. You'll go back. He says, it's a very good idea. 

That daughter dressed herself, you see, like for a Sunday outing. And I thought that-- how she looks like, it will be the best possible cover for me, that I am in their company. 

INT: How were you dressed? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: How were you dressed? 

MV: Just a-- a bit ragged, because of the-- I jumped the train, and I was a little bit-- I was-- 

INT: But what did you look like? Did you look like an officer? 

MV: Oh, yes, oh, yes. I looked like an officer. I was-- but I was dressed very-- very well, because I tore my jacket. You know, when I fell down-- when I fell down at the-- it was not-- it was not nice. I-- I looked like a peasant. I looked like a peasant boy. 

I was not an old man at that time. And that-- you know, I thought-- and I will avoid asking people the way to the station. They took me to the station. And I bought a ticket. I went-- I went-- in order to get, to be able to-- a line from the train, the station before Mielec, I had to change the trains in Przeclaw. 

I went down in Przeclaw, and I bought a ticket. It was for another train which moved through Mielec. And I could see this train arrived-- my train arrived with-- but instead of, let us say, 20 or 30 carriages, there were maybe four or five carriages, there. 

I could see this. My-- my sergeant-- my sergeant, who took my-- I could see him in the window-- I turned my head that he shouldn't see me, that he shouldn't see me. So that was how I came to Mielec. 

Then I came, and I came a station before-- about-- about seven kilometers before Mielec, I came down, and I walked. I knew that I didn't want to take a cart or something like that. I walked around seven kilometers, was how I came to town. 

And there we decided that the best way will be that I go to Cracow. There are a lot of Jews already coming from smaller towns, concentrating in Cracow. And there I will take a ticket. I'll take-- I will take over there a document that's registered in Cracow to go back to Mielec. 

And this is how it happened. I went to Cracow later, went to Mielec. From Mielec, I went by train to Cracow. From Cracow, I came back to Mielec, and this is how I started. 

INT: Why was the registration important in Crakow? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: Why was your registration important in Cracow? 

MV: Because people-- I had to be registered. And people should not know-- the people-- the-- the German people should not know that I came back from Soviet Russia. So I had to bring a document that I came back from Cracow, because you needed a so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. 

In order to be accepted, you had to get a [NON-ENGLISH], a document from Mielec that they accept you after you came from Cracow. So I had to have such a document, and I received such a document in Cracow-- that I lived in Cracow and I'm going back, and I'm going back to Mielec, and I came back to Mielec. 

And this is how-- how I start. I mean, it was a small town, and people lived there quite, quite nicely. They were very, very, very good conditions. People-- it was not a closed ghetto. It was open, and people were engaged in-- in trade. 

And they had their shops, like, you see, nothing has changed. The only thing which-- which-- which was changed-- it was you had to wear this white band with a yellow-- with-- with this Star of David, showing that you are a Jew. 

INT: Did you wear that band? 

MV: Yes. I wore one. I had to. I wore one. It, it didn't bother me. In the beginning, it didn't bother me. It didn't bother me, and I started working. 

INT: And where was the rest of your family? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: Where was the rest of your family? 

MV: The rest of my family, at that time, was in Przemysl, Wyszatyce, and in Kowel. 

INT: And your father was in? 

MV: My father was in Przemysl. My mother was-- my mother, with the two sisters, were in-- my mother and two sisters were in Wyszatyce. And later, and at a later stage, my father went to Kowel. 

The reason was my father changed place with my mother. My mother went from Wyszatyce-- my mother went from Wyszatyce to Przemysl. And my father went from Przemysl to Wyszatyce. 

And there he was with my-- with my sister, [NON-ENGLISH] Regina. And at that time, my sister in Wyszatyce and my brother-in-law were considered as Zionists and as capitalists. 

Of course, they, they had a factory making roof tiles. And the moment you had a factory, you were a capitalist, so they took away your, your-- they took away your factory. 

And they took away your house and place you in a slum, because you are a capitalist. And so they lived there in a slum. And there later, and my brother-in-law, [NON-ENGLISH] started to work as, as a roof tiler. 

He had to replace roof tiles on-- on the roofs. And later, they decided that they would-- the moment they got their passports, as workers, they decided to leave the town where they knew, they knew them as capitalists and as Zionists. 

So they went to Kowel, to live in Kowel. And they took my father with them and-- and my-- my-- my sister. They took them to Kowel with them. 

And it's interesting that my brother, who lives in Kowel didn't know about it-- that they are there for a time. But later they-- he didn't know that they are there. 

But later on, it so happened when they were-- my-- my-- my brother lives in Kowel, and he didn't know that my father moved to Kowel, because the father used to be in Wyszatyce. 

In the meantime, my brother was arrested with his family and with his father-in-law-- his whole family, because they registered themselves to go back to Poland, so they sent them over to Siberia. 

The train was sending there, and I learned later that my-- my sister came to the train, because it was not like they say, that the Poles say, that they were transported like the Jews to the gas-- to the gas houses. 

It's not true. They were quite comfortable in this-- in-- in-- in these carriages. They-- each carriage had a stove and had water. There was no-- was no problem. They, they traveled the same way as the Russian army traveled. 

But my sister came along and told them, look, you are going to Siberia. Everybody knows about that. Why don't you leave your children with me? There where Louise, and the boy, Wilhelm. 

They said, leave the children with me. She-- and the girl was 4 years old, and the boy was 8, 9 years old. She said leave them with me. I'll watch them, and I will-- I'll take care of them like my own children. 

But my sister-in-law and my brother said that where we go, our children go with me. And they didn't want to leave the children. That's a dangerous-- they go to Siberia. It gets cold. They said, look, they are going with us. 

And the fact was that my sister perished with her children, and my brother survived in Soviet Russia with, with his children, with his whole family. 

INT: And your father was still dressed in the traditional Jewish way? 

MV: Yes. There was no reason in Russia not to. There were Russians going with beards. It's how they looked, and so on, the traditional way. And they-- they stayed when the Germans came. 

And later, we decided-- they wrote us that they would like to come back to Mielec. Now, we-- through a middleman, we found a German officer-- a German, not officer, employee-- a German with his own car. And we paid him. 

He didn't want the money in advance, even. He said, when I bring your father and your sister, you will pay me. He was a very nice man, and he had to go by car to Kowel to bring two Jews. 

And then my father shaved his beard, But he still had a little beard. Shaved him, and he brought him. And he got him, and he said, he behaved in a-- in a-- in a-- in a way which showed that he had nothing to do with the Hitler regime. 

INT: Do you remember his name? 

MV: Beg pardon? I don't remember his name. I don't remember his name. He behaves in a very-- in what-- what-- was-- what-- what hit me to start with, he said, you don't need to pay me in advance. When I bring your father, you'll pay me. 

INT: What time of the year was this? 

MV: I don't remember. Couldn't-- 

INT: It's 1940. 

MV: That was definitely in 1940. At the end, could have been the end of 19-- it could have been the end of 1940. 

INT: So this was already a year after the war started? 

MV: No, no no, no. Just a second. It was not the end of '40. It was in '41. It was in '41, because at that time, the Germans were already in Kowel. And this is how he could have traveled to Kowel, because there were no more Russians in Kowel. 

Was-- must have been by the end of 1941. Not by the end, must have been maybe September, 1941. 

INT: So at this stage really, the war had been running for two years? 

MV: Beg pardon. 

INT: The war was going on for two years at this stage. 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: Did your father still have the same optimistic views about the end of the war? 

MV: No, I never discussed with him the optimistic views. I never discussed, I never discussed with him the optimistic views. But I don't know what happened to him. 

But when I ran away, before when even there was this-- when there was this talk about something's going to happen in Mielec, and I run away to Polaniec a day before-- was on Saturday. And on Sunday-- on Sunday morning, the [INAUDIBLE] let it be known through the remnants of the-- of-- through the acting president of the Judenrat, because the president of Judenrat went with me to Polaniec. 

He ran away with me to Polaniec. He let it be known that they were right-- there were something to be happened. But if he-- if-- if we collect so much and so much money, everything will be OK, like it was before. 

Well, the Jews collected some money. And everybody was-- was-- was full of joy that everything will be fine. And then it happened that my father came to my wife and told him, look, send somebody quick to Polaniec that Mark should be back as soon as possible, immediately. 

Because if not-- if it-- if it will-- if it will be known that he ran away, he may not be able to carry on as a solicitor. I can't understand it. He's tried to convince her that I should come back, because I will-- I broke the law running away. 

I was aware that something happened to his way of thinking-- that I will not be able to, to carry on as a solicitor-- but because I broke the law running away, I should come immediately back. 

No, it was-- I'd never had a discussion with him about his optimism, even when he could see what's going on. 

INT: And going back to the beginning of the war, was the outbreak of war a surprise for you and your family? 

MV: The outbreak of the war was not a surprise. People knew that something will happen. But people knew at the same time that something could be, that something may happen which will avert the war. 

[NON-ENGLISH] said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- we will not give an inch, we will not give a button from a-- from-- from-- from-- from-- from a coat. We will not give anything. But maybe there is something there will come. 

Something will happen to avoid the war, because we didn't believe that Hitler is-- at that time, is ready for-- is ready for a war. So we-- we were optimistic that something may-- may still happen to avert the war. 

INT: In that period when you came back to Mielec, summer, 1940, what did you do? 

MV: I was working as a solicitor. And by the way, I must have been the only solicitor in Poland working openly as a solicitor, because the Justice Department, which means the Department of Justice-- the Justice Department of the Generale Governement issued an order that Jews should not be allowed to practice. 

And a Polish solicitor is not allowed to employ a Jew, not even as a cleaner. Under no circumstances, should be employed, because they were afraid that under the guise of a cleaner-- of a cleaner, of a servant-- the Jewish solicitor, the Jewish lawyer will be working together with Poles. 

So that is when that was known. Therefore, when I came to Mielec, not a single Jewish solicitor was working-- was working. And I-- I contacted Antoni Droba, who was a partner of Leon Jacob Selpeter, my brother-in-law. 

And he was working in his premises. I contacted him if he could give some work to do outside, to-- to do illegally, but privately, from my room. 

And he answered to me, look, Freda Edda, that was, she was a secretary. There was a typist and secretary, she is working in the office, although she is not allowed to, working quite openly. 

And you come and work quite openly, and not a cock will [NON-ENGLISH]. 

INT: No rooster will crow. 

MV: Huh? 

INT: No rooster will crow. 

MV: No rooster will crow. People will think that you are allowed to, that you have got a permit. People don't know. Only solicitors. Only solicitors know that a Jew is not-- is not allowed to work in a solicitor's office. So you come along. 

When I came along next day-- the same day, when I came along, Droba took me to the court. We went to the-- we-- when-- when we entered the court, he said, take off this white band. 

And I took off the white band, and we went in the court. And I was greeted by-- this shows you there's antisemitism. I was greeted by the people all around-- by the solicitors, by the clerks, by the cleaners, by everybody like a friend. 

And as a matter of fact, I was introduced to one fellow, a tall, young fellow-- Dr. Frank. But he's not, not Frank, the-- it was another Frank. He's not, he had nothing to do with the Governor Generale, the, the Generale Governement. 

A doctor, he was a Reich worker working as a solicitor and barrister in Poland, at that time, in Mielec. But it so happened that he was the son of Dr. [INAUDIBLE] Frank, a surgeon, residing in Mielec, who came to Mielec before the First World War. 

He was respected by all the Jews in town, and he was in the best-- the best relationship with the Jews-- the Jews in town. His son went to school with Antoni Droba. They were school friends. 

But later he went back to Vienna to his relatives, where he practiced over there, and he married over there and became a Reich worker. Now, he came to Mielec, because you know, it's much easier for food and for all of those things to be together, to be in a small town like Mielec, where his father was the mayor. His father was the mayor of the town. 

So he introduced me to him. Ah, yes, I remember-- I went over there, and I had my armband still on. And it was this Dr. Frank that said, take it off. Here, in this court, between us, you are our colleague and our friend. And I don't want to see you with that here. 

INT: Thank you very much. We'll continue next tape.
